summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Practice and Procedure – Where the Court determines a contested interim application for valuation – Where the Respondent concedes that a valuation should occur – Where the Respondent suggests that her safety is at risk if the Applicant were to attend the former matrimonial home with the proposed expert valuer – Where the Respondent does not lead sufficient evidence to validate her allegations of safety risk at an interim stage –Where the Court makes consent orders permitting valuation to occur – Where the Court makes contested orders permitting the Applicant to attend the former matrimonial home with the proposed expert valuer

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

The matter before this Court relates to property only and is listed for hearing before me for four days commencing 28 April 2026. By way of background, the Applicant (“the husband”) and the Respondent (“the wife”) were married in 2007 and separated in December 2023. The husband is 52 years old and is an executive. The wife is 47 years old and is currently unemployed. There are two children of the marriage, X and Y, but the proceedings do not relate to them, even though, of course, they are highly relevant in any property proceedings. The children live with the wife and spend time with the husband.
The present application relates to C Street, suburb D (“the former matrimonial home”). It derives from the husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed 29 January 2026. It is supported by an affidavit of the husband dated 28 January 2026. The wife, who is currently representing herself, but apparently has recently received a grant of Legal Aid pursuant to s 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), has filed an affidavit dated 30 January 2026 (“the wife’s affidavit”).
It is trite that an application, even a discrete application like this, is decided on the basis of the evidence that is filed in the affidavits of the parties. Of course, in an interim application the evidence is not tested, nonetheless, it is the evidence filed upon which a Court makes a decision. The decision cannot be random. It cannot, for example, rely on evidence that is led from the bar table or told to the Court by the parties or their legal representatives during the course of the hearing.
There are a number of discrete issues relating to how, and who is to be present for, the purposes of a further valuation of the former matrimonial home. The first issue, and I sense this is the most important issue from the wife’s perspective, is whether or not the husband should be allowed to attend the former matrimonial home with Mr F (“the husband’s solicitor”) and Mr E (“Mr E”), the proposed expert valuer, for the purposes of accessing the waterfront.
The wife has expressed to me concerns about her safety in these circumstances. Indeed, she has raised a number of issues with me this morning, which are only alluded to in her affidavit in the most general terms. She is concerned about her safety and would therefore prefer that the husband not attend the property for the purposes of the valuation, except unless access occurs through the waterfront itself.
In practical terms, that means the husband would not be able to access the former matrimonial home from the street. He would have to find another access and then meet his solicitor and Mr E and direct them down to the waterfront.
That is opposed on behalf of the husband, who says that, firstly, an injunction is made by consent that neither the husband nor the wife approach the other during the inspection. In addition, the submission is made in his proposal and the husband’s evidence that he will at all times be accompanied by his solicitor, who, the Court notes is, a practitioner of much experience in this jurisdiction. As well as by Mr E, who likewise, is a valuer who frequently gives evidence in this Court. It is put that they are mitigating factors.
The wife conceded that even if the husband attended on the former matrimonial home, it would not be necessary for him to actually go through the former matrimonial home in order to access the waterfront. In addition, it is agreed that the wife’s present partner, Mr B, could be present at the time of the inspection.
The issue for the Court is whether these assurances about the wife’s safety are adequate in the circumstances. The Court has found, albeit not without some reluctance, that they are adequate, particularly in circumstances where the wife’s affidavit refers to her concerns in such general and, with respect, ambiguous terms. In those circumstances, and given the protections that are proffered, I am satisfied that the husband can attend the former matrimonial home as proposed.
There was a concern about whether Mr E would be able to take photographs of the inside of the former matrimonial home for valuation purposes. I accept the submission made by senior counsel for the husband that taking such photographs is standard valuation practice. I note that the existing valuer has taken photographs for the purposes of the valuation. Again, I note the wife’s concerns about privacy but, on balance, the valuation process needs to take place in accordance with standard practice.
I have made orders in relation to a process for the parties to resolve issues in relation to the valuation of any other assets, most of which, in accordance with the evidence, are items of personal property. I am satisfied that it is an appropriate way to deal with issues.
I make the observation that this is a case where there are clear issues of mistrust between the husband and the wife. I think that the wife would be assisted by legal representation to navigate these issues of mistrust, including her assertions of non-disclosure. At this late stage, and three months away from the final hearing, from the Court’s perspective, it is critical to do everything possible to make sure that the matter is ready.
